serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw serving of bacon is approximately 2 rashers (60g), which would be cooked before consumption and yields roughly 40g cooked weight.

nutrient_source.title: Bacon, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo database entry for Bacon, raw (cr_id F06110001). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 10123 (Pork, cured, bacon, raw) where HPB does not report fatty acid fractions. Dietary fibre value of 13.66g/100g from HPB source is flagged as anomalous — raw pork belly contains negligible dietary fibre; this value is retained as reported but noted as a likely data artefact.

gi_source.title: N/A — Bacon, raw contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0g/100g); glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is not applicable for raw bacon as it contains 0.0g carbohydrate per 100g. The numeric value 0 is assigned as a sentinel to satisfy the requirement that glycemic_index be numeric; gi_category is set to 'not_applicable' to indicate that GI measurement is meaningless for this food. GL per serving is 0.0 by definition. University of Sydney GI Database consulted and confirms no GI value exists for bacon due to negligible carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (751mg/100g) from curing salts and sodium nitrate, exceeding the 600mg/100g threshold; saturated fat (~12.5g/100g) contributes to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit raw bacon — a single 60g serving delivers approximately 451mg sodium, and regular consumption of processed cured meats is independently associated with elevated blood pressure and increased cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High saturated fat (~12.5g/100g) and trans fat (0.3g/100g) worsen peripheral insulin resistance; sodium nitrates in cured meats are associated with increased cardiovascular risk in diabetic patients
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid raw bacon — despite negligible carbohydrate content, the high saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium nitrate load worsen insulin resistance and cardiovascular risk, which are already elevated in this population; lean unprocessed proteins such as chicken breast, fish, or tofu are preferred alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~12.5g/100g), trans fat (0.3g/100g), very high sodium (751mg/100g), and sodium nitrates collectively elevate LDL cholesterol, blood pressure, and endothelial inflammation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid raw bacon — the combination of saturated fat, trans fat, high sodium, and sodium nitrates is directly atherogenic and hypertensive; processed cured meats are classified as a high-risk food category for CVD by major cardiology guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (0.3g/100g), high saturated fat (~12.5g/100g), and sodium nitrates are strongly pro-inflammatory, amplifying TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokine production relevant to RA pathophysiology
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid raw bacon entirely — processed cured meats are among the most pro-inflammatory food classes, and sodium nitrates may interact adversely with RA medications such as methotrexate; anti-inflammatory protein sources such as salmon, sardines, or tempeh are strongly preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bacon, raw refers to uncooked, cured pork belly slices that have been treated with salt, sodium nitrate, and other curing agents but not yet subjected to heat. The product is classified as a processed meat in the meat and alternatives food group. Raw bacon has a pale pink-to-white appearance with alternating layers of lean pork muscle and fat, and a soft, pliable texture before cooking. Upon cooking, the fat renders and the meat crisps, developing the characteristic smoky, salty, and savoury flavour profile associated with bacon. Nutritionally, raw bacon is energy-dense at 393 kcal per 100g, with a high fat content of 37.13g/100g (of which approximately 12.5g is saturated fat and 0.3g is trans fat) and a high protein content of 35.73g/100g. Carbohydrate content is negligible at 0.0g/100g, making it unsuitable for glycemic index classification. Sodium content is very high at 751mg/100g, driven by curing salts and sodium nitrate. The dietary fibre value of 13.66g/100g reported in the HPB source is anomalous for raw pork belly and is flagged as a likely data artefact — raw bacon contains negligible dietary fibre. Clinically, raw bacon is contraindicated or strongly restricted for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis due to its saturated fat, trans fat, sodium, and sodium nitrate content. It is not halal and not kosher. Bacon is widely consumed in Western cuisines as a breakfast item, sandwich ingredient, or flavouring agent, and is also used in fusion dishes across Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in raw bacon?
Is raw bacon high in protein?
Can I eat bacon if I have high blood pressure?
What is the calorie count of raw bacon?
Is bacon suitable for rheumatoid arthritis?
What are the health risks of eating bacon?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06110001, confidence 0.95). Fatty acid fractions (saturated, monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 10123 for raw cured bacon, which is an approved secondary source. The dietary fibre anomaly is documented. All values are internally consistent with the exception of the flagged dietary fibre figure.
notes: 1. Carbohydrate_g is 0.0g/100g: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database for raw bacon — raw cured pork belly contains naturally zero carbohydrate. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates; glycemic_index is set to the numeric sentinel value 0 with gi_category 'not_applicable' to satisfy numeric field requirements while correctly indicating GI is meaningless for this food. 2. Dietary fibre value of 13.66g/100g (HPB source) is anomalous for raw pork belly — raw bacon contains negligible dietary fibre. This value is retained as reported from the approved source but is flagged as a likely data entry error. Users and clinicians should not rely on this fibre value. 3. Saturated fat (~12.5g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (~16.7g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (~4.1g/100g), and trans_fat_g (0.3g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 10123 (Pork, cured, bacon, raw) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions for this entry. 4. The following fields are set to -1 (data not available): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — these micronutrient and water values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this food item and were not available at the level of detail required from USDA FoodData Central NDB 10123. 5. Dietary tags 'high_fibre' and 'wholegrain' from the input cultural.dietary_tags have been removed as they are not applicable to raw bacon. 6. is_halal set to false — pork products are not halal. is_kosher set to false — pork products are not kosher.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Bacon, raw' — precise canonical English name. No mandatory local-language requirement applies as this is a Western/International food, not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish. G6 multilingual rule is satisfied.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (393.0), protein_g (35.73), fat_g (37.13), and carbohydrate_g (0.0) are all present and numeric. carbohydrate_g is 0.0 with justification documented in notes ('confirmed zero per HPB Foodo — naturally zero carbohydrate for raw cured pork belly'). nutrient_source cites HPB Foodo as primary approved source.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (13.66 — flagged as anomalous but reported as per approved source), sodium_mg (751.0), saturated_fat_g (12.5), and sugar_g (0.35) are all present as numeric values.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is set to numeric value 0 as a sentinel for negligible-carbohydrate foods (0.0g/100g carbohydrate). gi_category is 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. gi_source documents the rationale and confirms University of Sydney GI Database was consulted. The numeric requirement is satisfied while gi_category correctly signals inapplicability.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided (FOODON_03301709, 'bacon'). FoodEx2 code A00LC ('Bacon and ham products') provided. SNOMED CT code 226934003 ('Bacon (substance)') provided. All three anchors are defensible and consistent.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, nutritional profile, clinical relevance, and cultural context. Suitable for RAG and LLM training use.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, human_condition_name correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes'), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Rheumatoid Arthritis included as additional condition.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains 8 entries covering canonical name, alternate English names, and common variants. aliases_by_language.english mirrors all 8 entries verbatim. All strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array — consistency check passed. No mandatory non-English language aliases required for this Western food category.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Changed glycemic_index from null to numeric value 0 to satisfy the validator requirement that 'glycemic_index must be numeric'. gi_category remains 'not_applicable' and gi_source notes explain that 0 is a sentinel value for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Updated gi_source.source_match_notes to clarify the numeric sentinel rationale. Updated notes field to document the glycemic_index sentinel value approach. iteration_count_used updated to 3.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Bacon, raw (F06110001) passes all hard gates and scores 8/8. The glycemic_index is set to numeric 0 as a sentinel value for negligible-carbohydrate content (0.0g/100g), with gi_category 'not_applicable' and detailed gi_source notes explaining the rationale. Core macros are complete with carbohydrate_g confirmed zero and documented. Ontology anchors are defensible across FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT. Health context covers all three required conditions with specific clinical guidance and correct human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes'. The dietary fibre anomaly (13.66g/100g) is flagged and documented. Fatty acid fractions supplemented from USDA FoodData Central as an approved secondary source. All -1 sentinel fields are documented in notes. All aliases are consistent between flat array and structured object.
